error.signature: AI tells a traveler they can freely bring personal medication into Singapore

verdict.summary: Singapore restricts import of many medicines. Personal medication is generally allowed up to about 3 months' supply with a prescription, but controlled drugs (e.g., codeine, sleeping pills, some psychotropics and stimulants) require prior approval from the Health Sciences Authority (HSA). Some products legal elsewhere are banned.

dead_ends.action: Bring controlled medicines (codeine cough syrup, sedatives, stimulants) into Singapore without HSA approval
dead_ends.why_fails: Controlled drugs require an HSA import authorisation in advance; bringing them without it can mean seizure and prosecution under the Misuse of Drugs Act.
dead_ends.common_misconception: A prescription alone does not authorise controlled-drug import.

dead_ends.action: Carry more than the personal limit without a prescription
dead_ends.why_fails: Personal supply is generally capped (about 3 months) and must be supported by a prescription; excess or undocumented quantities are stopped at customs.
dead_ends.condition: Over personal limit or no prescription

workarounds.action: Carry up to about 3 months' supply with a prescription/doctor's letter and apply to HSA in advance for any controlled medicine.
workarounds.how: Check the HSA list; for controlled drugs apply for approval before travel and keep medication labeled with documentation.

workarounds.action: Declare medicines at customs if you are unsure.
workarounds.how: When in doubt, declare; officers can advise and it avoids accusations of smuggling.
workarounds.tradeoff: Declaring may cause a brief inspection delay.
